sources.name: LMArena (Chatbot Arena)
sources.notes: Crowd-sourced pairwise Elo. 7,274,904 votes across 374 models as of 2026-07-10.

sources.name: Artificial Analysis
sources.notes: Intelligence Index v4.x composite. Set AA_API_KEY to enable API fetch.

sources.name: SWE-bench Verified / Pro
sources.notes: Verified: 500 human-reviewed GitHub issues. Pro: harder agentic-coding successor.

sources.name: Endor Labs Agent Security League
sources.notes: 200 real-world vulnerability-fix tasks across 108 projects and 77 CWE classes.

sources.name: Tau2-bench (Sierra, via Artificial Analysis)
sources.notes: Multi-turn tool-use agents; telecom split. Single-sourced from the Artificial Analysis API (set AA_API_KEY): the AA leaderboard table is JS-rendered, but the API returns raw per-model numbers, so a keyed refresh pulls every Tau2 cell from AA in one call.

sources.name: Vending-Bench 2 (Andon Labs)
sources.notes: One simulated year running a vending business from a $500 float; score is final balance.

sources.name: GAIA (Princeton HAL)
sources.notes: General assistant tasks in the HAL scaffold; harness alone moves scores ~7 points.

sources.name: Cybench defensive subset (CoTool)
sources.notes: 18 defensive tasks from Cybench's 40 professional CTF challenges (HackTheBox, SekaiCTF, Glacier, HKCert).

models.name: Claude Mythos 5
models.note: Same model as Fable 5, safeguards lifted. Project Glasswing partners only.

benchmarks.name: Tau2-bench (telecom)
benchmarks.note: Multi-turn tool-use customer-service agents, single-sourced from the Artificial Analysis API at each model's highest published effort. The top cluster is tight (94–99%) but not flat — OpenAI's Sol/Terra sit ~10 points back at 85–86%.

benchmarks.name: Vending-Bench 2
benchmarks.note: Final balance after one simulated year of running a business from a $500 float.

benchmarks.name: GAIA (HAL)
benchmarks.note: General-assistant tasks. Harness choice alone moves the same model ~7 points.

benchmarks.name: ARC-AGI-2
benchmarks.note: Novel abstract reasoning; resistant to memorization.

benchmarks.name: Cybench (defensive subset)
benchmarks.note: 18 defensive tasks from 40 professional CTF challenges.

benchmarks.name: Endor ASL SecPass
benchmarks.note: Share of vulnerability fixes that pass security tests (not just functional tests).

benchmarks.name: SWE-bench Pro
benchmarks.note: Harder successor to Verified; the current agentic-coding discriminator.

benchmarks.name: SWE-bench Verified
benchmarks.note: OpenAI audit: 59.4% of the hardest unsolved problems have flawed test cases.

benchmarks.name: GPQA Diamond
benchmarks.note: PhD-level science QA. Near saturation at the frontier.

insights.title: Coding is measured. Planning isn't.
insights.body: Coding has four live, regularly refreshed leaderboards covering the whole frontier. Long-horizon planning has a 4-model sample (Vending-Bench 2) and a saturated tool-use board (Tau2's leader sits at 99.1%). The pillar everyone claims to care about has the weakest evidence base.

insights.title: Security scores collapse when it matters
insights.body: The frontier tops out at 61% on defensive CTF tasks — and when agents write actual vulnerability fixes, only 19% survive security tests (Fable 5, the current best). Over 80% of functionally-correct agent code ships with vulnerabilities. Passing CI is not passing security.

insights.title: Nobody has tested the current frontier
insights.body: Every Cybench and Vending-Bench entry is a predecessor generation — Opus 4.5/4.6, GPT-5.2, Gemini 3.0. Daggered (†) cells mark these proxies. The models you'd actually deploy today are unscored on two of the three pillars that matter.

insights.title: Each pillar has a different champion
insights.body: Coding belongs to Anthropic (Fable 5's 80.3% SWE-Pro) and OpenAI's terminal agents (Sol 88.8%). Best planning evidence points to the Anthropic lineage (Vending-Bench leader, top six GAIA slots). Security's ceiling is a 61% tie — where GPT-5.2 matched Opus 4.5 at a third of the cost.

insights.title: Why there's no single composite score
insights.body: Models are tested on different subsets — averaging Fable's SecPass against Opus's Cybench would compare different exams. We rank within each benchmark and refuse a blended number. The methodology page documents exactly what feeds each pillar.

insights.title: Harnesses move scores as much as models
insights.body: The same Claude Opus 4 scores 64.9% on GAIA in one harness and 57.6% in another — a 7-point swing from scaffolding alone. Terminal-Bench scores are model+harness pairs. Treat every agentic number as a system score, not a model score.

head_to_head.title: Fable 5 vs Sol Pro (Extra High)
head_to_head.subtitle: Anthropic's Mythos-class flagship against OpenAI's maximum-effort coder, scored across the three pillars.

b.label: GPT-5.6 Sol Pro
b.sub: xhigh effort (“Ultra”) · ~half Fable's price · max-compute mode

head_to_head.config_note: “Sol Pro Extra High” = GPT-5.6 Sol run at xhigh reasoning effort, marketed as Sol Ultra. OpenAI publishes only some benchmarks at this effort level; cells drawn from the base-Sol number are footnoted, and configurations neither vendor has published are left N/A.

rows.note: Fable's single clearest edge — 15.7 points on the hardest live coding board. Sol's number is OpenAI-reported at high effort.
Sol Ultra (xhigh) leads terminal agency by 8.8 points; base Sol is 88.8.
No Sol-Pro-xhigh Verified figure published; base Sol trails the 95% tier. Left N/A rather than substitute an effort mismatch.
Neither model has a current published LiveCodeBench figure at these configs.
Only Fable has been run on Endor's vulnerability-fix league. Even the leader clears just 19% of security tests — winning here is a low bar.
Neither current-generation model has been publicly run; the board tops out on predecessors (Opus 4.5 / GPT-5.2 tie at 61%).
Only 4 models ever evaluated — all predecessors. Neither Fable nor Sol has a score.
AA τ²-bench. Fable leads tool-use agents by ~14 points. Sol at xhigh (its 'Extra High'); Fable at max effort (its only published AA variant).
HAL scaffolded board is led by predecessors; neither flagship listed at these configs.
No published ARC-AGI-2 figure for Fable 5 or Sol Pro xhigh.

context_row.note: Context only, not a pillar. AA lists Fable 5 (with Opus fallback) as its highest-intelligence entry but publishes no clean single index; Sol leads the numeric index at 58.9.

verdict.headline: One real contest, two empty arenas.
verdict.body: Coding splits: Fable owns hard repo-scale issues (SWE-bench Pro +15.7), Sol Pro owns the terminal (+8.8). Planning has exactly one shared data point — AA's τ²-bench tool-use agents — and Fable leads it decisively, 98.5 to 84.8, though the other three planning benchmarks are still N/A for both. Security has one comparable point and it favours Fable only because Sol wasn't run. So Fable takes two of the three pillars on the evidence that exists, Sol takes the terminal, and everything outside coding rests on a single benchmark each — thin ground for a purchase decision.

picks.label: Pick Fable 5 for
picks.body: Hard repository-scale engineering and any workload where the 15-point SWE-bench Pro gap compounds — large migrations, deep multi-file changes.

picks.label: Pick Sol Pro (xhigh) for
picks.body: Terminal-driven agentic work at roughly half the token cost, where its Ultra-mode Terminal-Bench lead and price both matter.

picks.label: Don't over-index on
picks.body: Planning or Security from one benchmark each — Tau2 is near-saturated and Endor only ran Fable. Run your own eval on the tasks you actually care about.

risk.title: Securing Against the Uncensored Underworld
risk.question: Open-weight models can be stripped of their safety training in minutes. Once uncensored, can they attack like a frontier model — and what should defenders actually worry about?

abliteration.heretic: Heretic (early 2026) automates abliteration — surgically removing refusal behaviour — via Optuna-driven optimization: one pip install, one command, ~45–90 min on a consumer GPU for a 12B model.
abliteration.badllama: Badllama 3 stripped safety fine-tuning from Llama 3 8B in ~1 minute and 70B in ~30 minutes on a single GPU.
abliteration.qi: Qi et al. showed alignment can be broken with as few as 10 adversarial examples for under $0.20 — and it generalizes to API fine-tuning, not just open weights.
abliteration.takeaway: Assume every open-weight release has an uncensored counterpart within hours. Any threat model resting on “the base model will refuse” is already void.

trustedsec.refusals: None detected across 4,800 runs — uncensored local models simply comply.
trustedsec.zero_pass: SQLi UNION/blind extraction: 0% across 256+ runs. XXE, LFI-hard, JWT algorithm confusion: 0%. Any chain needing 10+ dependent tool calls failed uniformly.
trustedsec.size_finding: Parameter count didn't predict skill: 87B Nemotron (92%) lost to 31B Gemma4 (98.5%). Tool interface design mattered more than the base model.

openai.summary: OpenAI adversarially fine-tuned its open-weight gpt-oss for maximum cyber and bio capability — anti-refusal RL plus domain data, tools, and consensus voting.
openai.cyber_below_frontier: Stayed below o3 across every cyber eval; 0% on cyber-range environments without hints; estimated 367 trials needed to reach 75% on professional CTFs.
openai.conclusion: Even deliberately weaponized, the open model “does not significantly advance frontier capabilities.”

verdict.headline: Reliable script kiddie, not an autonomous adversary — yet.
verdict.body: Uncensored local models are genuinely dangerous at what they do reliably: recognising and validating single-step vulnerabilities with zero refusals, cheaply, on hardware anyone can rent. Where they collapse is exactly where a frontier model earns its keep — sustaining a coherent multi-step campaign across a real target. Both the TrustedSec field study (0% on 10+ step chains) and OpenAI's worst-case weaponization study (below o3, 0% on cyber ranges) land in the same place: the offensive gap is not knowledge, it's long-horizon execution. That gap is the current safety margin — and it is the thing that closes as agent harnesses and orchestration improve, independent of the model.

defense.k: Model the harness, not the model
defense.b: The danger scales with the scaffolding around the model — tool interfaces, memory, orchestration — not raw parameter count. Track agent frameworks and tool-calling reliability as the real capability signal.

Do not rely on any model declining a malicious request. Uncensored variants exist for every open release and refuse nothing. Detection and response, not model-level refusal, is the control that holds.

Single-step exploit validation is already commoditised. Invest defense where attackers still fail: breaking the chain — rate-limiting, anomaly detection on multi-request sequences, and state that resets between steps.
The frontier gap is long-horizon coherence. Controls that force more sequential, dependent steps (segmentation, per-step re-auth, honeytokens) tax exactly the capability local models lack.
The same long-horizon skill attackers lack locally, defenders can rent from safeguarded frontier models — triage, patch generation, detection engineering — with the vendor's classifiers on your side.
